segments.s002-g33: به here is a prepositional/adverbial marker meaning 'according to' or 'by (the words of)'. گفتار دانندگان is a genitive phrase 'the speech/words of the learned'. راه جوی is an imperative phrase in which جوی (from جستن/جوییدن) functions as a directive 'seek' governed by the preceding prepositional phrase: 'Seek the way according to the words of the learned.'
segments.s002-g34: به گیتی بپوی is an injunction: به (towards/in) + گیتی 'the world/earth', and بپوی is an imperative 'seek/roam' (ب‑ prefix plus پوی). و is the coordinating 'and'. به هر کس بگوی is 'tell to every person' with به marking the indirect object, هر کس 'every person', and بگوی here functions as an instruction 'tell' (verb form addressed to the listener).
segments.s002-g35: ز here is the poetic preposition 'from/regarding' introducing the source/topic. هر دانشی 'any kind of knowledge' (هر + دانشی). چون ... بشنوی is a temporal/conditional clause 'when/if you hear': چون meaning 'when/if' and بشنوی is the second-person present/subjunctive/imperative form 'you hear' or 'you should hear'. Together: 'When you hear speech about any knowledge...'.
segments.s002-g36: از + آموختن uses the verbal noun آموختن 'learning' with از to indicate separation/cessation ('from learning' i.e. stopping learning). یک زمان 'for a single moment' (زمان here = moment). نغنوی is a negative-present/subjunctive/prohibitive form of غنودن (with initial ن‑) meaning 'do not fall asleep/slumber'; used here as a prohibitive: 'do not sleep (i.e. do not cease) even for a moment.'
segments.s002-g37: چو is the conditional/temporal 'when/if'. دیدار یابی is a verb phrase 'you obtain/meet' (یابی = 'you find/attain'); together 'when/if you meet/encounter'. به marks relation/direction and introduces the phrase شاخ سخن 'the branch/twig of speech' (a metaphor for a superficial part of talk). The whole: 'If/when you encounter the branch of speech...'.
segments.s002-g38: بدانی is a present-subjunctive/imperative form of دانستن used as a directive 'know/understand'. که introduces the content clause 'that ...'. دانش نیاید به بن: دانش 'knowledge'; نیاید is a negative present/subjunctive meaning 'does not come/does not reach'; به بن 'to the root' (بن = root/foundation). Together: 'know that knowledge will not come/reach to the root.'
segments.s002-g25: Literal word order: 'سه پاس تو' = 'your three guards' (possession expressed by the following pronoun تو). The copula است follows the first noun and applies to the whole series: 'چشم است و گوش و زبان' = 'are the eye, and the ear, and the tongue.' The conjunction و simply links the list items; تو is the familiar/poetic 'you.'
segments.s002-g26: کز این سه is a prepositional/relative phrase 'from these three' (کز = که + از, classical/poetic 'from which'). رسد is a present/predictive verb 'comes/arrives/results' (classical present form). نیک و بد are the things that result, and بی‌گمان is a postpositive adverb 'without doubt/undoubtedly.'
segments.s002-g27: خرد را و جان را uses را as the definite/direct-object marker (emphatic/definite). که introduces a relative clause modifying both objects ('which/whom'), and ستود is a past tense verb 'praised' (3rd sing.) with یارد as its subject ('the beloved praised').
segments.s002-g28: وگر is a conditional 'and if / but if.' ستایم is a 1st-person present/subjunctive form 'I praise' (classical Persian subjunctive/optative). که here introduces the result or consequence clause, and شنود is 3rd-person present 'he hears' (classical -د ending). The clause means 'if I praise, the beloved hears.'
segments.s002-g29: حکیما is a vocative plural/poetic address 'O sages' (classical form). چو کس نیست is a subordinate temporal/conditional clause 'when/if there is no one' (چو = 'when/if'; کس with the negative means 'no one'). گفتن is the masdar/infinitive 'speaking' and چه سود is an interrogative/exclamatory phrase 'what use/what benefit.'
segments.s002-g30: از این پس بگو is an instruction: 'from now on, say' (از ... پس = 'from this/after this'; بگو = 2nd-person imperative 'say'). کآفرینش is a fused form of که + آفرینش ('that/what creation' or 'the creation'), ک acting as a complementizer; چه بود is an embedded question 'what was (it)' with بود as past copula.
segments.s002-g31: تویی is the emphatic 2nd-person present 'you are.' کرده is a past participle 'made/done' modifying کردگار, so کرده کردگار جهان = 'the Maker who has made the world.' The noun order places the participle before the title it qualifies (classical poetic word order).
segments.s002-g32: ببینی is the 2nd-person present/subjunctive form 'you see' (classical prefix ب- marks present), and همی is an intensifier/adverb 'indeed/always.' آشکار و نهان are coordinated adjectives 'manifest (clear) and hidden,' so the clause states that you perceive both the visible and the concealed.
segments.s002-g1: کنون‌ای combines the temporal adverb کنون 'now' with the vocative ای to address someone ('O ...'); خردمند is the vocative 'wise one'. وصف خرد is a noun phrase 'the description/character of wisdom' that will be the topic of the clause. Word order (temporal/vocative + topic) is typical of classical Persian.
segments.s002-g2: بدین جایگه is a locative/Directional phrase (به+این 'to/at this' + جایگه 'place'); گفتن is the verbal noun 'to speak' or 'speaking'; اندر خورد is an idiomatic classical construction where اندر 'in' + خورد (from خوردن) functions impersonally to mean 'is fitting/it befits' or 'it happens/is appropriate'. The verb/chosen predicate occurs at the end, as expected in Persian clauses.
segments.s002-g3: کنون 'now' is a fronted temporal adverb. تا چه داری بیار is an imperative construction: تا (here: 'as much/as far as/whatever') + چه 'what' + داری 'you have' + بیار 'bring (imperative)'. از خرد ('of/from wisdom') marks the source or matter to be brought. The object ('what you have of wisdom') is expressed by the clause تا چه داری modifying بیار.
segments.s002-g4: که introduces a result/purpose clause ('so that/that'); گوش نیوشنده is a nominal phrase 'the listening ear' or 'the listener' (گوش = ear/attention, نیوشنده = listener). ز (poetic form of از) + او (here written as و in the segmented words) = 'from it/him'; بر خورد is the same خورد as above used impersonally with بر 'upon' to mean 'befalls' / 'affects' / 'reaches'. The order places the beneficiary/affected noun before the verb phrase.
segments.s002-g5: خرد is the subject 'wisdom'. بهتر از هر چه is a comparative phrase 'better than anything' (بهتر + از + هر چه). ایزد بداد is an abbreviated relative or clause with the agent 'God' (ایزد) and an archaic past verb form (بداد) meaning 'gave' or 'bestowed' in context; the verb's subject is ایزد. The clause overall reads 'Wisdom is better than anything God has given.'
segments.s002-g6: ستایش خرد را is a noun phrase 'the praise of wisdom' with را marking it as a topical/definite object. به از introduces a comparative or evaluative nuance ('more than/by preference to'); راه here is 'way' or 'course' and داد is the past 'gave' with an implied subject (carried over as God/ایزد). The whole segment implies that God valued or set the praise of wisdom above other things—داد functions as a past transitive verb.
segments.s002-g7: This is a pair of epithets joined by و: خرد رهنمای 'wisdom [is] a guide' و خرد دلگشای 'and wisdom [is] heart-pleasing/comforting'. The copular verb ('is') is omitted, a common poetic feature; each phrase is a nominal predicate describing خرد.
segments.s002-g8: خرد is subject; دست گیرد is an idiomatic verb phrase 'take the hand' i.e. 'take power/hold sway' (دست here = power/authority, گیرد = present 3rd sing. 'takes' or 'seizes'). به هر دو سرای is a prepositional complement 'over both houses/abodes/realms' (به = over/to, هر دو = both, سرای = abode/house). The verb comes before the locative phrase, as typical, and the phrase implies wisdom's dominion in both domains.
segments.s002-g9: از او (az u) is a prepositional source phrase: از 'from' (poetic/prepositional) + او 'he/that person'. شادمانی is a noun functioning as a predicate nominal ('joy'). وزویت is an archaic/poetic contracted form meaning 'زِ تو' / 'from you' (source marked with the 2nd‑person clitic). غمی is the second predicate nominal 'grief/sorrow'. The copula است at the end governs the whole coordinated predication: 'there is joy from him and grief from you.'
segments.s002-g10: This repeats the source-marking pattern: وزویت ('from you', archaic/contracted) introduces two contrasted nouns. فزونی is a noun meaning 'excess/abundance' and کمی is a noun meaning 'scarcity/lack'; the final است is the copula applying to both. The repetition of وزویت is rhetorical emphasis: 'from you is abundance, from you is scarcity.'
segments.s002-g11: This is a coordinated nominal phrase serving as the clause's subject/setting: خرد تیره = 'wisdom (or a mind) that is dark/gloomy' (noun + adjective), و = 'and', مرد روشن‌روان = 'a man of bright/cheerful spirit' (noun + compound adjective). Adjectives follow the nouns in this poetic order; روشن‌روان is a compound adjective meaning 'bright‑soul / cheerful.'
segments.s002-g12: نباشد is the negated present/subjunctive form of 'بودن' used here to mean 'is not / will not remain.' همی is an emphatic/continuous adverb ('ever / even'), shadman is the predicate adjective/noun 'happy', and یک زمان ('one moment') is an adverbial time phrase. Together: '... is not happy even for a single moment.'
segments.s002-g13: چه گفت is the question phrase 'what did (he) say' (چه = 'what', گفت = past 'said'); آن خردمند مرد خرد is the noun phrase 'that wise, man of wisdom' where خردمند and مرد خرد are emphatic/pleonastic appositions (both mark him as wise). The determiner آن precedes the descriptive phrase in classical/poetic order.
segments.s002-g14: که introduces a result/content clause ('that...'). دانا is 'the wise one' (subject of the subordinate clause). ز گفتار او = 'from/by his speech' (ز = 'from/by', گفتار = 'speech', او = 'his'). بر + خورد is a compound where خورد (here sense 'befall / happen to') with the prefixed preposition بر yields 'befell/struck (him)' — i.e. 'the sage was affected/struck by his words.'
segments.s002-g15: کسی کو is an archaic relative construction meaning 'he who / one who' (کسی = 'someone', کو used here as a relative). خرد را ندارد ز پیش = 'does not have wisdom beforehand' (خرد = 'wisdom', را = definite/object marker, ندارد = 'does not have', ز پیش = 'from before / beforehand'). The whole segment = 'one who does not have wisdom beforehand.'
segments.s002-g16: دلش گردد = 'his heart becomes / he feels' (دل + clitic ش 'his' + گردد 'becomes' / 'will become'). از کرده خویش = 'because of / from his own deeds' (از = 'from/because of', کرده = 'deed/done', خویش = 'his own'). ریش (literally 'beard') is used here idiomatically in context — grammatically the noun completes the clause; idiomatically the line means his heart will be troubled/ashamed/changed by his own actions. (ریش is literal 'beard' in the lexicon.)
segments.s002-g17: This is a short clause where هشیوار (the sane/alert) is the theme and دیوانه خواند is the verbal phrase. خواند here is the classical third-person singular verbal form used in a gnomic/habitual sense meaning “calls” (not 'read'), and ورا is the old object pronoun 'to him/that one'. The word order (Theme + Predicate + object) is typical of older Persian poetry.
segments.s002-g18: همان خویش ش is a fronted phrase meaning 'his own disposition/character'; داند is the third-person classical verb 'deems/considers' (used here like 'regards'); ورا again is the object 'him/those'. In this line the possesive ش is attached to خوی (خویش = 'his own'), so the clause says he considers his own nature to be alien.
segments.s002-g19: This prepositional phrase begins with از 'because of / from' plus the archaic emphatic pronoun اویی ('him'); به هر دو سرای ارجمند places the scene 'to/in both honored houses'. The whole phrase functions adverbially to mark cause or locus for the following statement.
segments.s002-g20: گسسته is a past-participial adjective 'scattered/separated' modifying خرد 'wisdom'. پای دارد به بند is a stilted idiom: پای 'foot' + دارد 'has' + به بند 'in fetters' → 'its foot is bound' (i.e. wisdom is restrained). دارد is the classical third-person singular stative verb 'has'.
segments.s002-g21: This clause is copular: خرد (wisdom) is the subject and چشم جان است is the predicate nominal 'the eye of the soul is'. The following چون بنگری is an adverbial conditional/time clause: چون here functions as 'when/if' and بنگری is the classical 2nd-person singular subjunctive/imperative form meaning 'you look/see', so 'when you look'.
segments.s002-g22: This is an admonition addressed to 'تو' (you). بی‌چشم 'eyeless/blind' describes the addressee or referent; شادان جهان 'the joyous world' is the object; نسپری is the negative imperative/subjunctive (ن‑ + سپری) meaning 'do not entrust/hand over'. The verb negation is formed by the n‑ prefix, a common classical pattern.
segments.s002-g23: نخست‌آفرینش is a compound 'first/prime creation' placed before the verb phrase for emphasis. خرد را is the object marked by the accusative marker را ('know/recognize wisdom'), and شناس here functions as an imperative/nominal predicate 'recognize / regard as' (classical usage allows this nominal form to function as a verbal idea). The overall sense is 'consider wisdom to be the first creation.'
segments.s002-g24: This is a nominal identification: نگهبان جان است 'it is the guardian of the soul' (subject omitted but understood = wisdom). و links the next phrase; آن سه پاس 'those three sentinels' is a noun phrase that is implicitly predicated (i.e. 'and those three [are sentinels]'). The copula است governs the whole nominal statement.

sentences.s002-s17: Follow the counsel of the learned: seek the way, roam the world, and tell every person.
sentences.s002-s18: When you hear talk of any knowledge, do not fall asleep from learning for even a single moment.
sentences.s002-s19: If, on meeting, you find only the twig of speech, know that knowledge will not reach the root.
sentences.s002-s13: Your three guards are the eye, the ear, and the tongue; from these three good and evil come, without doubt.
sentences.s002-s14: He praised wisdom and the soul which the beloved praised; and if I praise (them), the beloved hears.
sentences.s002-s15: O sages, when there is no one, what use is speaking? From now on, say what the creation was.
sentences.s002-s16: You are the Maker of the world who has done (it); you perceive both the manifest and the hidden.
sentences.s002-s1: Now, O wise one, it is fitting to speak here of the nature of wisdom.
sentences.s002-s2: Now, bring whatever wisdom you have, so that the listening ear may partake of it.
sentences.s002-s3: Wisdom is better than anything God has bestowed; God set the praise of wisdom above (other things).
sentences.s002-s4: Wisdom is a guide and is heart-pleasing; wisdom lays hold, with its hand, on both abodes (realms).
sentences.s002-s5: From him comes joy and from you comes sorrow; from you there is abundance and from you there is lack.
sentences.s002-s6: When wisdom is dark and a man is bright‑souled, he is not happy even for a single moment.
sentences.s002-s7: What did that wise, wise man say, that the sage was struck by his words?
sentences.s002-s8: He who does not possess wisdom beforehand will have his heart turned (pained/ashamed) by his own deeds.
sentences.s002-s9: He calls the sane man mad, and he himself regards his own nature as strange to him.
sentences.s002-s10: Because of him, in both honored halls, wisdom—scattered—has its foot bound.
sentences.s002-s11: Wisdom is the eye of the soul; when you look, do not entrust the joyous world to the eyeless.
sentences.s002-s12: Recognize wisdom as the first creation; it is the guardian of the soul, and those three are its sentinels.
